text: The robotic setup for the extended right colectomy is shown, with ports placed at an oblique angle for better access to the distal transverse colon. The surgeon uses a tip-up fenestrated grasper to place tension on the ileocolic pedicle. The superior mesenteric vein is identified through the mesentery, and cephalid retraction is applied while scoring over the ileocolic vein and SMV interface.

The surgeon continues anterior dissection along the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) up to the confluence with the ileocolic vein, ensuring a safe plane without anterior branches. Medial to lateral retromesenteric dissection exposes the duodenum. The surgeon identifies and dissects an early branch of the right colic artery from the ileocolic artery, noting its variability. The ileocolic vessels are circumferentially dissected and ligated using a vessel sealer, taking care not to impinge on the SMV to avoid thrombosis.

After ligating the vessels, the surgeon drops the duodenum and head of the pancreas from the transverse mesocolon using a medial to lateral approach. Dissection continues along the superior mesenteric vein, exposing the gastrocolic trunk and middle colic artery. The middle colic artery is dissected at its origin for oncologic nodal resection and will be ligated. The retro mesenteric dissection enters the lesser sac from the inferior approach, facilitating further dissection. The gastrocolic trunk is addressed, and the superior colic right vein entering the gastrocolic trunk is delineated and divided.

The surgeon identifies the right gastroepiploic vein entering the gastrocolic trunk, which is then divided. The anatomy of the gastrocolic trunk is variable, making identification challenging. After ligation, dissection continues to communicate into the lesser sac.

The surgeon continues medial to lateral dissection to detach the remaining hepatocolic attachments. The gastrocolic ligament is separated from the superior border of the transverse colon, entering the lesser sac. Routine lateral paracolic dissection is performed to complete mobilization and release the small bowel mesentery from retroperitoneal attachments. The ureter is visualized and protected. The small bowel mesentery is ligated with a vessel sealer, and the terminal ileum is transected using a robotic sixty blue load stapler. The colon transection point is identified, and ICG is used to confirm perfusion before the colon is transected with a robotic stapler.

The freed specimen is placed in the right upper quadrant. An isoparastolic intracorporeal anastomosis is performed, with the enterotomy made at a safe distance from the staple line. The stapler is inserted but not fully deployed to sixty millimeters. Stay sutures are placed on each end of the common enterotomy to aid exposure and minimize spillage. The closure is completed with a running V-lock suture, imbricated back on itself, using a vicryl suture as a landmark to ensure closure of the distal incision. The specimen is exteriorized through a fan and steel incision.
His final pathology demonstrated a stage three colon cancer as well as an advanced adenoma. I appreciate you watching the video. Please leave comments and follow us at MCC Gastrointestinal Oncology.
